narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 37 and an undergraduate GPA of 2.90, you've put together a thoughtful application for law school, including your middle-income family background and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and it estimated a 55.5% chance of failure for you—high enough that it tipped the decision toward rejecting your application to prioritize students with stronger projected outcomes. The key factor driving this prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers and strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar exam struggles, outweighing other positives in your profile. Your LSAT score, slightly above the average of 36.7, pushed in the opposite direction by suggesting good potential for success and helped lower the failure odds somewhat. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, though below the typical 3.21 average, still contributed positively by showing you have a foundation that could support passing the bar. Compared to typical students in the dataset—who often rank around the middle in both Year 1 (average decile 5.71) and Year 3 (average 5.53)—your lower Year 3 performance stood out as the biggest concern, making your overall profile riskier in the model's eyes. While your full-time status aligns with most applicants (over 92% of whom go full-time), it didn't sway the decision much here. This isn't a judgment on your character or effort, John—it's just the model highlighting patterns from past students where early academic dips like yours correlated with bar challenges. To move forward, focus on boosting your recent grades to climb those school deciles, perhaps through targeted tutoring or extra coursework, and consider retaking the LSAT if you can push it higher. Strengthening these areas could flip your predicted odds in a future application. Many students in similar spots have rebounded by addressing weak spots head-on—reach out to admissions advisors for personalized strategies, and keep pursuing your goals with persistence.
